name: Portet-d'Aspet Pass – emblematic passes of the Pyrenees
name_original: Circuit 5 : Le col de Portet-d’Aspet — Les cols emblématiques des Pyrénées
description:
The Col de Portet-d'Aspet is a classic of the Pyrenees and a monument of the Tour de France. Since 1910, it has been used more than thirty times by the big loop. A link between the Ger and Bouigane valleys, it culminates at 1,069 meters above sea level.

The start is in the town center of Saint-Girons, a remarkable and colorful mountain village. The beginning of the route follows a greenway that more or less runs along the Salat before embarking on a less busy road. Relatively hilly, it leads to the Col de Larrieu which is a good warm-up before undertaking the major ascent of the loop.

A short descent takes you to the village of Aspet. From there, you progress on a false flat rising at the bottom of the valley. From the Oule bridge, the slope becomes steeper and you soon pass in front of the stele in homage to Fabio Casartelli. Around kilometer 50, you finally reach the Portet-d'Aspet pass with a few passages at more than 13%.

Savor the calm that reigns there before embarking on a beautiful descent. The Bouigane and Lez rivers are responsible for guiding you to your starting point in Saint-Girons.

name: Mente Pass – emblematic passes of the Pyrenees
name_original: Circuit 8 : Le col de Menté — Les cols emblématiques des Pyrénées
description:
The Menté is one of the most used passes by the Tour de France in the Pyrenees. Since 1966, the big loop has taken it twenty times. Linking the Ger valley to that of the Garonne, it rises to 1,349 meters above sea level.

The start of the loop that leads to this classic starts from the charming mountain village of Fronsac.
About ten kilometers at the bottom of the valley allow you to warm up before attacking the steep slopes of the pass. Indeed, the average slope is 9% over a little over nine kilometers with some passages beyond 11%. The last part of the climb is done by means of several switchbacks. At the top you will discover a stele in memory of Serge Lapébie, a former professional cyclist.

The descent requires a certain vigilance because of the many laces that wind through the forest. At the Oule bridge, the slope becomes gentler and you move through a remarkably peaceful valley bottom. You follow the Ger for a long time before leaving it to attack the small Col de Buret. You then go on to the Col des Ares, a little longer, which is the last ascent of the route. The descent then brings you back to the heights of Fronsac.

name: Alpera: Snow Well, Mills Route, and La Cruz Loop
name_original: Pozo de Nieve, Ruta de los Molinos y la subida al Vertice Geodesico de La Cruz de Alpera — circular desde Alpera
description: Hike a difficult 12.0-mile (19.2 km) circular route from Alpera, exploring the Pozo de Nieve, Ruta de los Molinos, and panoramic views from

name: Wine and Fish Route: Lekeitio Loop
name_original: Orilla del río Lea – circular desde Lequeitio por el Faro de Santa Catalina
description: This difficult 19.6-mile touring cycling route from Lekeitio offers coastal views and a visit to the Santa Catalina Lighthouse.

name: Pailhères Pass – emblematic passes of the Pyrenees
name_original: Circuit 14 : Col de Pailhères — Les cols emblématiques des Pyrénées
description:
The Col de Pailhères or Port de Pailhères is an emblematic passage in the Pyrenees. Culminating at 2,001 meters above sea level, it stands as a magnificent link between the resort of Ax-les-Thermes and the Ariège gorges. The Tour de France used it for the first time in 2003.

The start of this great loop is at the Ax-les-Thermes SNCF station. The mountain resort has the advantage of being as attractive in winter as in summer. The route begins by immediately climbing to the heights of the city. It continues until you reach the Col du Pradet which is a great first ascent. After a few bends downhill you come across the Rébenty which flows down the valley. He will be the guarantor of a magnificent section that wanders through beautiful gorges for about thirty kilometers until reaching the Aude.

You then dive into new gorges, those of the Aude this time. They take care of taking you to Usson-les-Bains on a slight uphill false flat. This is where the climb officially begins towards the Col de Pailhères. Relatively regular, it has about twenty hairpin bends that are above the Mijanès-Donezan station. The last part of the climb includes passages around 10%.

At the top, enjoy the magnificent panoramas that unfold before you before falling into a superb descent. This one also has a few switchbacks but much less than the other side. Gradually you dive into the valley until you find your starting point in Ax-les-Thermes.

name: Sanctuary of Oro Loop from Murgia
name_original: Santuario del Oro — circular desde Murgia por el Valle de Zuia
description: Cycle a moderate 15.4-mile loop from Murgia to Santuario del Oro in Valle de Zuia, enjoying challenging climbs and panoramic views.

name: GR 38: Wine and Fish Route Loop
name_original: Lagunas de Laguardia – circular desde Elciego
description: Cycle the moderate 16.4-mile Lagunas de Laguardia loop from Elciego, exploring vineyards, wetlands, and historic towns in Rioja Alavesa.

name: Pagoeta Natural Park Loop from Orio
name_original: Parque Natural de Pagoeta – circular desde Orio por Aia
description: This difficult 15.6-mile touring cycling route through Pagoeta Natural Park offers coastal views and significant elevation gain.

name: Portillon Pass – emblematic passes of the Pyrenees
name_original: Circuit 7 : Col du Portillon— Les cols emblématiques des Pyrénées
description:
The Portillon is a classic of the Pyrenees and an emblematic pass of the Tour de France. Culminating at 1,293 meters above sea level, it marks the border between France and Spain. Since 1957, it has been used about twenty times by the runners of the big loop.

The route starts at Bagnères-de-Luchon train station. This historic spa town enjoys a unique reputation in the valley. It is located at the foot of the pass, at the gates of Spain. A small flat portion allows you to warm up before entering the valley of Pique. This is where the main kilometers of ascent to the pass begin.

Along the way, the Sidonie waterfall offers a burst of freshness in the hollow of a bend formed by the road. The climb continues in the heart of a beautiful forest which ends up leading to the pass. By tilting downhill you now enter Spain in the beautiful Aran Valley. It ends with four wide bends before entering the village of Bossost.

Now the rest of the loop is much flatter until the finish. You benefit from a long downward false flat which leads back to the border which is not far from the village of Fos. You continue along the Garonne on a cycle path to Saint-Béat. You conclude by finding the valley of Luchon to Bagnères.

name: Tour of Biscay: Urdaibai Coast Loop
name_original: Elanchove y Ea – circular desde Gautéguiz de Arteaga
description: This difficult 21.6-mile touring cycling route from Gautéguiz de Arteaga explores coastal villages and the Urdaibai Biosphere Reserve.

name: Eskuinaldea - Uribe Kosta Trail
name_original: Getxo city tour – circular
description: Cycle the Eskuinaldea - Uribe Kosta Trail, a moderate 11.7-mile touring route with coastal views, charming villages, and historical landmark

name: Port de Balès – emblematic passes of the Pyrenees
name_original: Circuit 13 : Port de Balès — Les cols emblématiques des Pyrénées
description:
Culminating at 1,755 meters, the port of Balès is less well known and renowned than its neighbours, which are none other than the Tourmalet or the Portillon. However, its irregular and brittle ascent has earned it the chance to be climbed a few times by the Tour de France since its first in 2007.

The route starts at Bagnères-de-Luchon train station. This spa town is the starting point for many passes in the same area. The first part runs on a long false flat which joins the Garonne which you then leave in the direction of SIradan. You now progress on a slightly steeper road to Mauléon-Barousse.

The climb officially starts in the village and stretches for almost 19 kilometres. The first are affordable before the slope becomes more demanding and irregular. The road rises via several switchbacks and some passages exceed 14%. The arrival at the top gives you a beautiful view of the surrounding peaks which exceed 2,000 meters in altitude.

The descent plunges you into the bottom of a valley where the Neste d'Oueil flows. This pretty stream brings you back a few kilometers from Bagnères-de-Luchon, which you just need to reach via a slightly downhill section.

name: Col d'Azet – emblematic passes of the Pyrenees
name_original: Circuit 9 : Col d’Azet — Les cols emblématiques des Pyrénées
description:
The Col d'Azet is less known and frequented than its prestigious neighbours. However, since 1997, it has already been used ten times by the Tour de France. Located in the Louron valley between Saint-Lary-Soulan and Loudenvielle, it culminates at 1,580 meters above sea level.

The start of the course is in the center of Saint-Lary-Soulan. A false downward flat begins by bringing you to the pretty mountain village of Arreau. Then you start a long false flat going up to Génos and its lake. This is where the ascent to the Col d'Azet starts.

The climb is just over seven kilometers long and includes some passages at more than 13%. This one has several switchbacks that quickly take you above the valley and its lake. The Col d'Azet culminates at 1,580 meters and therefore offers a remarkable landscape on its slopes as well as at its summit.

The descent is made on the foothills of a beautiful rocky ridge to the small town of Azet. Several switchbacks meander under the village before reaching the village of Saint-Lary-Soulan.

name: Sorginetxe and Aizkomendi Dolmens Loop
name_original: Dólmenes de Sorginetxe y Aizkomendi – circular desde Agurain/Salvatierra
description: Cycle an easy 14.4-mile route from Agurain/Salvatierra to explore the ancient Sorginetxe and Aizkomendi dolmens in Llanada Alavesa.

name: Vasco-Navarro Greenway and Estibaliz Sanctuary Loop
name_original: Santuario de Estibaliz  – circular desde Vitoria-Gasteiz por la Vía Verde del FFCC Vasco-Navarro
description: Cycle the 29.4-mile Vía Verde del FFCC Vasco-Navarro from Vitoria-Gasteiz to the Sanctuary of Estibaliz, a moderate family-friendly route.

name: Tierra de Pinares and Portillo
name_original: Tierra de Pinares y Portillo — circular desde Cuéllar
description: This 61.5-mile (99.0 km) moderate road cycling route from Cuéllar takes you through the pine forests of Tierra de Pinares.

name: Alto de la Bobia - Oscos Loop
name_original: Vegadeo y Alto de Garganta – circular desde Taramundi
description: Tackle the difficult Alto de la Bobia - Oscos Loop, a 41.6-mile road cycling route with 5302 feet of elevation gain through Asturian mountai

name: Peñas del Viento Loop
name_original: Nuviana y Peñas del Viento – circular desde San Juan de Piñera
description: Tackle the difficult 31.8-mile Peñas del Viento Loop road cycling route, featuring 4777 feet of elevation gain and sea views.

name: Peñas del Viento via Argatón Loop from La Arena
name_original: Peña de los Vientos por Argatón – circular desde La Arena
description: Tackle the challenging Peña de los Vientos por Argatón circular road cycling route from La Arena, featuring steep climbs and panoramic coast

name: Senda del Oso and Puertos de Marabio Loop
name_original: Puertos de Marabio – circular desde Proeza
description: This difficult 49.7-mile road cycling loop combines the Senda del Oso's green valleys with the challenging climbs of Puertos de Marabio.

name: Pic du Midi d'Ossau and its lakes – running in the Ossau Valley
name_original: Le pic du Midi d'Ossau et ses lacs — Courir dans la vallée d'Ossau
description:
This superb loop of 23.1 kilometers and 1,550 meters of elevation takes you around the Pic du Midi d'Ossau, making you discover its most beautiful lakes.


The start of this route in the Ossau valley is in the Bious-Oumettes car park. From there, you climb to the Col de Suzon passing by the lake of Bious-Artigues and a beautiful valley made up of wide grassy areas.

At the pass, you switch towards the refuge of Pombie where you join a variant of the HRP (Haute Route Pyrénéenne). At first, it takes you up to the Col de Peyreget, before descending in laces on the Ossau plateau to the foot of Castérau.

Gradually, you climb around the peak to Lake Bersau nestled under the peaks of Hourette and Larry. From this pretty water point, you only have a long downhill stretch that begins with Lake Gentau and its breathtaking view of the Pic du Midi d'Ossau. Here, you find the GR 10 that you do not leave until you return to your starting point. Along the way, you will discover the Miey and Roumassot lakes.

name: Tour of the Pic du Midi d'Ossau – running in the Ossau Valley
name_original: Le tour du pic du Midi d’Ossau —  Courir dans la vallée d’Ossau
description:
With this route I suggest you go around the star of the valley, the Pic du Midi d'Ossau. Nicknamed "Jean-Pierre", this emblematic summit of Béarn is located in a remarkable and preserved natural site. Intermediate and experienced trail runners discover the beauty of these landscapes through a loop of around 17 kilometers and 1,100 meters of elevation gain.

The route begins on the Bious-Oumettes plateau where there is free parking accessible by car.
A path rises from it and gives you quick access to the artificial lake of Bious-Artigues. From the lake, a gradual climb takes you to the Col de Suzon crossing a magnificent valley which reveals the first peaks of your route.

From the pass, the slope becomes gentler and you then unroll on a pleasant false flat to the refuge and Lake Pombie nestled under the summit of the Pic du Midi d'Ossau. From the refuge, you join a variant of the HRP (Haute Route Pyrénéenne) which guides you to the Col de Peyreget, the highest point of the route at 2,313 meters above sea level.

This passage offers superb views of the surrounding peaks. Enjoy it before embarking on a long downhill section. The first part includes a few bends that lead to the plateau of the Ossau valley where you join the GR 10 which accompanies you to your starting point.

name: Camin de Careses Loop
name_original: Pola de Siero – circular
description: This easy 13.2-mile (21.2 km) road cycling route around Pola de Siero offers green landscapes and town views in Asturias.

name: Great tour of the peak – running in the Ossau Valley
name_original: Le grand tour du pic — Courir dans la vallée d'Ossau
description:
Passes, ridges and lakes are on the program for the 28.8 kilometers and 1,600 meters of elevation gain that form a grand tour of the emblematic Pic du Midi d'Ossau. With this loop, I suggest a route that varies both the types of terrain and the landscapes to discover.

The adventure starts in the free car park of Bious-Oumettes from which you quickly reach the lake of Bious Artigues. The gradual climb begins in the forest then leads to beautiful grassy expanses before ending at the Col de Suzon. You switch in the direction of the refuge of Pombie before prolonging the descent more curtly which ends at the Gave du Brousset.

At the level of the river, the path rises again, passing gradually on the sides of several peaks located on the Franco-Spanish border. On the outskirts of the Pic d'Astú, you begin a beautiful portion of the ridge that leads you to the Pic des Moines, dancing on the border. The highest point of the route with its 2,349 meters above sea level, this summit offers an exceptional panorama over the Ossau valley and the Spanish side.

From the peak, you successively discover several lakes which offer a unique view of the Pic du Midi d'Ossau which you have already largely skirted. Shortly after the last lake, you enter a beautiful forest following the GR 10. Follow the markings of the path which will take you to the starting point in the Bious-Oumettes car park.

name: Gourzy loop – running in the Ossau Valley
name_original: Boucle de Gourzy — Courir dans la vallée d’Ossau
description:
This beautiful loop on the heights of the town of Eaux-Bonnes is ideal for practitioners looking for a significant kilometer/elevation ratio. Indeed the 14.5 kilometers include almost 1,200 meters of elevation gain.

The route begins in the car park of the casino in the thermal village of Eaux-Bonnes. You start on a wide track which you leave quickly to descend into a district of the village. This section allows you to warm up well before undertaking a relatively steep climb. This one meanders in a beautiful forest while climbing in the gorges of Balour. It stops for a moment to reveal a magnificent grassy plateau which allows you to breathe a little before continuing the climb.

Gradually the forest opens up and you begin to see several peaks of Le Gourzy that you are about to discover. A final steep climb in the Combe de Balour takes you to the highest point of the outing: the Pas de Brèque. Located at the foot of the Pic de Gourzy at 1,866 meters, it offers a magnificent panorama of the surrounding peaks.

The descent then begins towards the wood of Assouste. Savor the view one last time before plunging into the forest following a trail that forms numerous switchbacks to your starting point.

name: Loop of Orteig – running in the Ossau Valley
name_original: La boucle d'Orteig — Courir dans la vallée d’Ossau
description:
This course owes its name to the guide and hunter Jacques Orteig. His walking abilities were known throughout the Ossau Valley. The loop begins in the car park of the casino of the spa town of Orteig's birthplace: Les Eaux-Bonnes.

The route begins by following the Sourde stream immersed in a beautiful wooded area. This part is done uphill until you leave La Sourde. Then it's a beautiful flat area that takes you to the heights of Gourette.

You then join the GR 10 and follow its markings towards Lake Anglas then Hourquette d’Arre. Nestled at 2,574 meters above sea level, this pass marks the highest point of your outing and your return to the Ossau valley.

The beginning of the descent is quite steep before softening when you reach the foothills of the Petite Arcizette. You leave the GR 10 to embark on the last climb of the route towards the Col de Lurdé. A pretty path takes you to Pas de Brèque in an absolutely remarkable setting.

Enjoy the unobstructed view of the valley one last time before following a track that plunges into the woods of Assouste. Many switchbacks fork in this beautiful forest before bringing you back to your starting point.

name: The soum of Grum – running in the Ossau Valley
name_original: Le soum de Grum — Courir dans la vallée d’Ossau
description:
Grum soum owes its name to a mixture of ancient local dialects. Soum comes from Occitan and designates the summit while brum, crum means "thick clouds" in Gascon. It could therefore be translated as “the summit that catches the clouds”. This beautiful loop suggests going around it before finally climbing to its summit.

The starting point is in the Col d'Aubisque car park. This myth of the Tour de France is an interesting crossing point of the Pyrenees. It stands as a real one in the middle of remarkable valleys. The route starts with a pleasant flat area that runs under the soum of Grum. A slight climb looms ahead of you before the trail dips towards Labourd's hut.

About a kilometer further ahead is the main climb out of the exit. This follows a superb ridge which has no difficulty. Gradually, it guides you to the foot of soum de Grum. At the cost of a last portion of ascent, you reach its summit. This remarkable belvedere rewards your efforts by offering a breathtaking panorama of the surrounding peaks, including the Ger massif.

Go back slightly before plunging into a short descent that brings you back to your starting point.

name: Sanctus – running in the Ossau Valley
name_original: Le Sanctus — Courir dans la vallée d’Ossau
description:
The start of this course is in the ski resort of Gourette. Attached to the village of Eaux-Bonnes, this station is crossed by the GR 10. It also offers several accessible trails.

The route starts directly on the route of the GR 10 going up to the foot of the Pène de Sarrière. A little before the second kilometer, the track leaves the GR to embark on a steep climb that leads to the Louesque lakes. Located in the circus of the same name, these three small lakes suggest a nice break.

A final, gentler uphill section takes you to the highest point of the outing, Sanctus Peak. Perched at an altitude of 2,482 meters, it forms a magnificent belvedere over the Pic du Ger and the many lakes below.

Finally, a beautiful descent takes you back to your starting point. It wanders along the shores of Lake Uzious before returning to the GR 10 a little before that of Anglas. Follow its markings until you reach Gourette.

name: Arre-Sourins – running in the Ossau Valley
name_original: L'Arre-Sourins — Courir dans la vallée d’Ossau
description:
Often neglected in favor of its emblematic neighbour, the Pic du Ger, Arre-Sourins is a pretty summit located on the heights of Gourette. The start is in the ski resort in a car park near the GR 10.

The route starts by going up on the GR path towards Lake Anglas. This first lake of the day offers an unobstructed view of the Arre-Sourins. The climb continues by meandering in a scree on its last portion. At the top, at 2,614 meters, you find yourself at the highest point of the exit. You will discover a magnificent panorama of all the emblematic peaks of the Ossau valley as well as many lakes. Take advantage of this superb belvedere before turning back.

Back on the Arre plateau, you go around the peak of Anglas to head towards the lake of Lavedan then that of Uzious. A few kilometers further you find the GR 10. Follow its markings until you find your starting point in Gourette.

name: Tour of Cézy – running in the Ossau Valley
name_original: Le tour de Cézy — Courir dans la vallée d’Ossau
description:
The peak of Cézy stands as a citadel watching over access to the bottom of the Ossau valley. This route invites you to go around it starting from the thermal village of Eaux-Chaudes.

Leaving the village you embark on an ascent that begins in a beautiful forest. As you climb, the landscape opens up to vast summer pastures. Behind you, the view also reveals superb peaks such as the Bouerzy peak or the Montaut peak. A last section of steep climb leads to the Pas de Brèque.

The Pic du Ger now stands in front of you. A pretty balcony path guides you to the Col de Lurdé. Located at an altitude of 1,958 meters below the peak of Cézy, it is the highest point of your outing.
You continue by starting a beautiful downhill portion that joins the GR 10 path. A beautiful balcony suspended above the Soussouéou valley leads you to the Lacaya plateau.

The end of the route is on a pleasant path that winds through the forest while descending towards the Eaux-Chaudes. Along the way, do not miss the incredible cave which is a few kilometers before returning to your starting point.

name: Great tour of the lakes – running in the Ossau Valley
name_original: Le grand tour des lacs — Courir dans la vallée d'Ossau
description:
This course of 49 kilometers and 3,400 meters of elevation gain starts from the ski resort of Gourette. This large loop is ideal for all lovers of panoramas and mountain lakes. Along different valleys, it has its share of surprises and spectacular viewpoints.

The route begins by taking the GR 10 which climbs slightly in the valley. The climb intensifies when you leave the GR to climb to Lac de Louesque. Shortly after, it is the Sanctus peak which provides the first panoramic point of your outing. You continue on a long descent which passes by the Uzious pass, then the Bouleste hut before ending at the Lac de Tech. There follows a clear portion of ascent which leads to the Col de l'Hospitalet, the highest point of the route with its 2,523 meters of altitude.

A brief descent leads to Lake Migouélou and its refuge, ideal for a well-deserved break. A new climb offers you the Col d'Artouste and its breathtaking view of the Pic du Midi d'Ossau before switching towards the Lac d'Artouste. You will find a restaurant near the arrival of the train. Take the opportunity to eat and fill up with water.

A beautiful portion of descent allows you to unwind as you go down to the Soussouéou valley. At the huts of the same name, you embark on the final ascent of the route. This leads to Hourquette d'Arre following the GR 10 path. At the pass, you switch to Lake Anglas. All you have to do then is find your starting point by continuing to follow the red and white markings of the GR.

name: Saldaña Castle and Virgen del Valle Sanctuary Trail
name_original: Ruinas del Castillo de los Condes Saldaña — circular por los prados de Saldaña
description: Hike a moderate 7.7-mile (12.4 km) circular route around Saldaña, exploring historic castle ruins and scenic meadows.

name: Río Pisuerga Riverbank Loop
name_original: Puente de Torquemada — circular a orillas del río Pisuerga
description: An easy 4.1-mile (6.5 km) circular hike along the río Pisuerga, featuring the historic Puente de Torquemada and diverse riverine scenery.

name: From Portets to Bordeaux
name_original: De Portets à Bordeaux
description:
The south of the Gironde is full of slopes and singles just waiting to be explored. In 20 minutes by train, you are already far from Bordeaux and only have to think about the return trip. On the left bank of the Garonne, the drop is relatively flat and you can enjoy this outing at the pace of a walk through the pines at the start and then the vines finally.


When you get off the train, you go through the vineyards towards Saint-Selve, then enjoy a shaded road under the pines. The roads being mainly straight lines, keep a close eye on the route to exit and join the first gravel portion. This leads first to a DFCI and then to a small path that crosses a small stream. Once out of this path, you arrive at the town of Civrac and take the road towards La Brède, if you feel like it, there are small cafes and terraces that will allow you to enjoy the outdoors during beautiful days.

Small difficulty, leaving La Brède, you go through a small road that climbs a little, the advantage is that this road is not very busy compared to the EuroVélo 3 which could take you to Bordeaux. You cut through the forest to reach the Route de la Solitude, the road on which the Domaine de la Solitude is located, a Pessac-Léognan renowned in the region.

You then head towards Martillac to take the old road that leads you to Bordeaux. You arrive at the station where you can either get back on the train or set off to explore another route offered in this Collection.

name: Castillo de la Mota Loop
name_original: Castillo de la Mota — circular por Astudillo
description: This easy 4.1-mile circular hike around Astudillo features the historic Castillo de la Mota and unique underground dwellings.

name: From Cérons to Bordeaux
name_original: De Cérons à Bordeaux
description:
Many people like to reach Saint-Émilion by bike, on this route, I suggest you come back instead. The most famous village in Bordeaux is indeed accessible by train and it is very easy to get there to visit a cellar or two and return with small provisions in your bags.

The vines as far as the eye can see make you discover the Dordogne which passes at the foot of Saint-Émilion. The roads are winding and tortuous, offering at each bend a view of wine estates renowned by their appellations.

Don't forget that you have only traveled a few tens of minutes by train to find lost roads and hidden paths to discover the practice of gravel.

After the locality of Curton, a path spans the Canedone on a small, excessively bucolic stone bridge.

The wine roads make their way to Biron where you cross the clearing between forest and meadows.

The Roger Lapébie track then leads to Lignan-de-Bordeaux. Take the time to stop at the Bistrot de la Pimpine, located on the edge of the Lapébie trail, it is a real landmark for cyclists.

The descent to Bordeaux is made by crossing the wood of Loc Boué, a small haven of peace at the foot of Bouliac.

You find Bordeaux, to pick up a corkscrew at home and open these pretty bottles that are in your bags (in moderation, of course!).

name: Les Praeres and Alto de La Campa Loop
name_original: Alto La Campa y Les Praeres – circular desde Villaviciosa por la comarca de la Sidra
description: This difficult 38.7-mile road cycling route from Villaviciosa features the challenging Alto La Campa and epic Les Praeres climbs.

name: Bolaños de Calatrava from Almagro
name_original: Bolaños de Calatrava desde Almagro – circular por Moral de Calatrava
description: An easy 27.5-mile road cycling route connecting Almagro, Moral de Calatrava, and Bolaños de Calatrava, featuring historical towns and La Man

name: Vía Verde de La Camocha and San Andrés Reservoir Loop
name_original: Embalse de San Andrés de los Tacones por la Vía Verde de La Camocha – circular por Gijón
description: This moderate 17.6-mile road cycling route from Gijón explores the Embalse de San Andrés de los Tacones via the scenic Vía Verde de La Camoc

name: Villanueva de la Fuente, Montiel, and Albaladejo Loop
name_original: Montiel desde Villanueva de la Fuente – circular por Albaladejo
description: This moderate 26.8-mile (43.1 km) road cycling route in Campo de Montiel connects Villanueva de la Fuente, Montiel Castle, and Albaladejo.

name: Campo de Montiel Castles Loop
name_original: Castillo de Montiel desde Albaladejo – circular por Torre de Juan Abad
description: Explore the historic Campo de Montiel on this 43.9-mile road cycling route, linking Albaladejo, Torre de Juan Abad, and Castillo de Montiel.

name: Santa Inés and Montenegro Passes Loop
name_original: Puertos de Santa Inés y Montenegro — circular entre las Sierras de Urbión y Cebollera
description: This difficult 43.2-mile (69.6 km) road cycling loop tackles the Santa Inés and Montenegro Passes with 5479 feet (1670 metres) of climbing.

name: Avilés and Candás Loop
name_original: Embalse de la Granda, Cndás y Mirador de La Formiga – circular desde Avilés
description: This moderate 23.1-mile road cycling route from Avilés explores the Asturian coast, Embalse de la Granda, and Mirador de La Formiga.

name: From Cestas to Bordeaux
name_original: De Cestas à Bordeaux
description:
This first route is the most accessible. Only about twenty kilometers separate you from the center of Bordeaux from Cestas-Gazinet station. Count about ten minutes of travel time by train.

Once at the quay in Cestas, you head towards the Bois des Sources du Peugue, it is a little frequented wood where you will have the joy of taking a small footbridge to pass on the island in the middle of the lake. In this park there is a children's playground, large picnic tables. Ideal if you are planning a day trip with children for example!

The program continues, after the sources of the Peugue you take the direction of the Forest of Bourgailh and its park. For the record, this park is a former rehabilitated waste collection center. Here, you can climb to the top of the wooden towers which make great observation posts, or try your hand at rock climbing or the various pump tracks.

You return to Bordeaux along the Peugue, sheltered from cars and prying eyes. The Peugue is a small tributary of the Garonne, which passes under the streets of Bordeaux.

Even if twenty kilometers may not seem like much, there is a lot to see on this little outing. After the Bourgailh forest, the Bois du Burck and its BMX track welcome you to have a little more fun.

You finally cross the vineyards of Château Haut-Brion before returning to the city after this short getaway just a few pedal rides from Bordeaux.

name: Cañón del Río Lobos and San Leonardo de Yagüe Loop
name_original: Río Lobos y San Leonardo de Yagüe — circular por el Parque Natural Cañón del río Lobos
description: Moderate 48.9-mile road cycling route through pine forests and the dramatic Cañón del Río Lobos, with varied terrain.

name: From Langon to Bordeaux
name_original: De Langon à Bordeaux
description:
On this last course, we are on something that looks like an adventure. Fording, sand, roots, castles and other curiosities are on the program.

Langon is a dormitory town on the outskirts of Bordeaux. It is therefore very well served by train. You leave Langon by the south going up towards Sauternes, known for its eponymous wine. The next section goes down to Budos and its medieval castle. The roads are winding and after Landiras you taste the first joys of the hidden paths along the road.

Saint-Morillon has an excellent little café called “À l’improviste”, warm and friendly, it is perfect for enjoying a refreshment or a hot drink depending on the season.

Once past La Brède, a small surprise trail awaits you. On the program of sand and water, something to delight your transmissions, but above all, something to find a child's soul on his bike. The broom of the planes of the aerodrome is then heard

The end of this route literally cuts through the woods and fields to find Bordeaux, passing some beautiful properties in the Pessac-Léognan appellation. Since there are only a few kilometers left, do not hesitate to stop for a visit, or a tasting, in moderation to finish the trip safely.

name: On the heights of Ax-les-Thermes
name_original: Sur les hauteurs d’Ax-les-Thermes
description:
This loop of around ten kilometers leads to the summit of Sarrat de l'Étang at an altitude of 1,127 metres. Along the way, you will discover the rock of the Virgin, Lake Campauleil and the village of Orgeix before returning to your starting point in Ax-les-Thermes.

Located in Ariège, Ax-les-Thermes is a mountain village with character. Its tourist offer includes a spa, a summer resort, an excursion center and a winter sports resort. Something to satisfy sports and nature lovers all year round.

The route starts from the Ax-les Thermes train station which is on the Pyrenees line of the Occitanie Rail Tour. Take advantage of the center of the village to find the lunch that you will savor higher up in the mountain.

You start by climbing on the rock of the Virgin which constitutes a beautiful belvedere on the village. You embark for a pretty plateau where the lake of Campauleil is located. This beautiful body of water is nestled in the hollow of the mountains. You go up along its peaceful banks until you reach Orgeix.

From the village begins a sharp ascent towards the Sarrat de l'Étang. Along the way, remember to turn around to appreciate the beautiful view of Orgeix. At the top, the vegetation opens up to offer a vast panorama over the Oriège valley.

You close this loop with a pleasant path that wanders through the forest before leading to Ax-les-Thermes. Relieve your feet in the Roman baths before taking the train again!

name_original: Parque Joaquín Acacio, Ermita del San Cristóbel, Molino de Estremera — circular en Villarrobledo
description: Tackle a difficult 13.3-mile hike in Villarrobledo, exploring Parque Joaquín Acacio, Ermita del San Cristóbel, and Molino de Estremera.

name: Pibeste peak from Lourdes
name_original: Le pic du Pibeste depuis Lourdes
description:
This generous 23 kilometer loop covers several peaks around Lourdes which offer their share of panoramas over the heart of the Pyrenees chain. Pretty mountain trails are responsible for making the link between each of them.

World famous for its historical religious heritage, Lourdes attracts many pilgrims every year who come to meditate there. The city is placed at the gates of the Gaves valley. It extends from Lourdes at 325 meters above sea level, to Vignemale culminating at the Spanish border at 3,298 meters.

The departure is located at the Lourdes train station which is on the Piedmont line of the Occitanie Rail Tour. You join the Gave de Pau where the GR 101 path crosses, you will follow the markings to the village of Ossen. Take advantage of your crossing of the city to find your lunch. Indeed, when leaving Lourdes, you will find nothing on the way to refuel. Also bring enough water.

From the village, you embark on a climb that leads to the peak of Alian, the first belvedere of your journey at 1,089 meters above sea level. The ascent continues towards the Pic du Pibeste via a pretty ridge passage. Arrived at the top, the panorama on the chain of the Pyrenees seems infinite. In clear weather it is possible to observe a significant number of peaks.

The descent begins by sliding between the soum of Mont Agut and the mall of Arréou before reaching the village of Ségus. From there you undertake a new and final climb to Béout. This summit offers a beautiful view of the city of Lourdes that you are about to find. Back in the city, take a short detour through the area of the sanctuary of Lourdes before returning to the station.

name: Peñas Cinglas & Coda Sartén
description:
This is one of my favorite tours in Aínsa. It consists of a combination of the officially signposted rounds number 5 (Peñas Cinglas) and number 18 (Coda Sartén). The proportion of single trail is 80 percent, which will delight downhill fans and uphill trail fanatics.

Let's start in the parking lot in Aínsa. As soon as you have left the town behind you, the first climb begins, which initially has a pleasant gradient, but then gradually becomes more challenging and changes from a wide gravel road to a demanding single trail in the last few meters to the Partara summit. Once at the top, you can take a deep breath and enjoy the fantastic view of Aínsa.

Well rested, you take on the first downhill of this round under your cleats, which takes you winding and with technical refinements to the riverbed of the Ena. Now it's time to pedal uphill again. With a lot of power in your legs, you master this strenuous passage called “Sendero Solans de Caballera” with flying colors. The trail descent that follows will quickly make you forget the hardships and put a smile on your face.

Then you wind uphill again, first on a narrow path and then on an asphalt road until you turn onto a single track near a small cemetery. This path continues uphill and is steep and technically demanding. After you have circled the hill called Magdalena, there is a fun downhill that really puts you in a good mood and spits you out again at a former monastery at the entrance to Boltaña.

The last notable climb of this round begins in Boltaña, which takes you to a very special highlight: a section called Coda Sartén. This trail is one of the most famous in the entire Zona Zero. You roll through spectacular gorges, where you should have a lot of courage. Since the Enduro World Series was a guest here, some of the particularly fall-prone areas have been secured with a wooden fence. Once you have mastered this section, you roll across meadows and fields back to the starting point of the tour.

name: Trilogía de San Vicente
description:
The trilogy starts in Aínsa and takes you to three great singletrack descents, all of which are located near the town of San Vicente. The names of these paths are: Planaermita, Cocullón and Mayorcas. They are entertaining, fast, rocky and really fun. There is a fountain in San Vicente where you can fill up your water along the way. The route is suitable for everyone who likes moderately difficult singletrack descents and likes to complete the climbs on wide gravel paths and asphalted side roads.

If you are looking for a particularly challenging single trail tour, then you can add the “Triología de San Vicente” to the Peñas Cinglas and Coda Sartén tours (routes numbers 5 and 18). But be careful, this mountain bike tour is really tough. All uphills are extremely strenuous and will really challenge you, we promise! This mega round is almost 47 kilometers and 1,850 meters in altitude and - depending on your fitness - takes around five to six hours.

name: Journey to the Underworld
name_original: Viaje al Inframundo
description:
Are you ready for the underworld? Perfect! Because this tour is called a “journey into the underworld”. And not without reason. Forgotten mountain villages, lonely mountains, pristine trails and great views await you on this remote route.

The chances of you meeting other people on this tour are very low. Not only because there are hardly any people who live in this area, but also because the route is quite strenuous. In total you climb around 1,700 meters and some of them are really tough. So bring enough snacks with you to recharge your batteries in between.

Once you reach the deserted mountain village of Morcat, you will be rewarded again and again with magnificent views of the Pyrenees. Then you continue uphill until you reach the highest point of the tour at around 1,300 meters above sea level just below the Capramonte summit - a great spot for a breather!

Then things go downhill rapidly and you quickly realize that you have now arrived in the underworld. Speed lovers will like this descent, which requires your full concentration due to the large stones. The path is a bit overgrown in places, which underlines the remoteness of this descent. This is followed by undulating sections, some of which run on single trails and others on wide forest paths. Only when you hit the road again at kilometer 40 will the underworld be behind you.

This is followed by an asphalted climb to Santa Maria de Buil and a little later you come to the - at least in my opinion - the most scenic and technically beautiful section of this round: a trail called Sarrastaño. Tight curves, challenging steps and an enchanting rocky landscape ensure a lot of driving fun. There is another very challenging climb up the Partara and an entertaining descent on the Partara Express Trail and you are back in Aínsa.

name: Bajo Peñas (variant)
name_original: Bajo Peñas Variante
description:
Below Aínsa's striking local mountain, the Peña Montañesa, there is a wide network of great single trails that can be combined with each other as desired. With this tour I will show you a variant of the Bajo Peñas Route, which is also known locally as route number 7. This variant is a little more strenuous, as you pedal almost eight kilometers longer and end up with almost 250 meters more elevation on the clock.

It starts in Aínsa. The first climb leads over quiet side streets, beautiful gravel paths and wonderful single trails. Anyone who enjoys technical trails uphill will love the route to San Beturián. Shortly afterwards there is a downhill to Los Molinos, which leads spectacularly through a unique canyon.

At Los Molinos you will then come to an asphalt road that will take you up to the town of Oncins. If you want to replenish your energy here, you can stop at a small restaurant and treat yourself to local delicacies. Shortly behind the town, you then turn back onto a trail - well strengthened - that winds further up and is very challenging in places.

At around kilometer 18 you have reached the highest point of this tour. Your downhill fun begins on a trail called Canal Mayor. Full concentration is required here because the path is steep, rocky and riddled with roots. Pumped full of adrenaline, the path spits you out onto a wide gravel road.

After you have passed the town of Torrelisa, the trail fun continues. The descent feels extremely long because there is always a steep uphill climb every now and then. At the end of the Panille Trail there are once again magnificent views of the Peña Montañesa, which at 2,291 meters is the landmark of this area.

name: Viewpoints of Aínsa and Morillo by Cerro Cotón
name_original: Miradores de Aínsa y Morillo por cerro Cotón
description:
Fancy a first-class single trail tour that is challenging but not too long? Perfect! Then take a closer look at this route. It starts in Aínsa, runs 80 percent on wonderful single trails and also takes you to two fantastic viewpoints. Sounds tempting, right?

Let’s start in Aínsa. On the way to Morillo de Tou you climb a few meters in altitude, cycle through fragrant pine forests and over spectacular rocky cliffs until you reach the Torre de Cotón. Here you have to stop and enjoy, because the view of the Mediano reservoir and the Cinca Valley is simply wonderful. Another entertaining singletrack descent and you have arrived in Morillo de Tou. The next milestone is the climb to the summit of Partara, from where you have sensational views of Aínsa, the Ara River and the central Pyrenees.

Once you've had your fill of this great sight, what follows is a quick downhill that doesn't have any major obstacles, but does have a great route along a spectacular rock face. Once you have crossed the Ena riverbed, you return to the starting point in Aínsa via asphalted side roads and gravel paths.

name: Virgen de La Sierra
description:
On this wonderful route you explore several top trails that are great both uphill and downhill. The uphill trails are long, steep and tricky, which is why you should be in very good shape and have sophisticated riding technique.

In addition to the classic trails called Sarrastaño and Partara Express, the highlights also include pretty places such as Guaso or the church in San Martín del Buil. The latter is located on a hill and can be seen from afar. If you have some time and leisure, it's worth taking a detour to this striking building from the 11th century.

Then you take the Sarrastaño Trail under your tunnels, one of the most beautiful paths in Zona Zero. The sensational panorama of the Pyrenees peaks and the trail surface ensure a very special bike experience that you won't soon forget. By the way, the subsurface consists of coin-sized stone lenses and fossils of former single-celled organisms - known in technical jargon as “nummelites”.
